trajectory_summary: Kelly arrives as a warm, motivated, technically-informed buyer with an immediate need and a large future solar project on the horizon. Holland books the appointment but leaks energy steadily through two supervisor holds, two misapplied qualification steps, and a product-naming error at close. The booking is fragile: Kelly is self-directing to the website, expressed urgency for same-day help that couldn't be met, and received no verbal handshake or no-driving anchor.
outcome_dispute: The call ends with a booked appointment, so a 'booked' outcome tag is technically accurate. However, the booking quality is low: the button-up is incomplete (no 'is that fair' handshake, no no-driving/at-computer anchor), the closer may need to walk back a product Holland named that Ion does not carry (Enphase), and Kelly's self-service pivot at close signals reduced confidence in the appointment delivering what she needs. This should be flagged as a conditional/fragile booking, not a clean sit.

primary_coaching_focus.topic: Scope discipline under product pressure — and the Enphase accuracy problem
primary_coaching_focus.quote: I'm not exact here. I can actually look and see. So I know that we have, like, Enphase batteries, and then we have a Tesla Powerwall, and then, there's some other ones. I just can't remember what they're called.
primary_coaching_focus.why: Holland named a product Ion does not carry (Enphase) when Kelly pressed for specifics. This is not a minor scope-creep miss — it creates a false expectation the closer must correct before the consultation can begin. The codex's escape hatch ('that's exactly the kind of question our specialist is built to answer') was available and would have served Kelly better. The manager needs to address both the scope discipline habit and the specific product accuracy error in this 1-on-1, and flag the Enphase mention in the handoff brief.

key_moments.quote: I'm building a home, so I will be, getting a solar system for my much larger home, about a 3,500 square foot home.
key_moments.stewart_read: Kelly volunteers a near-term full residential solar project — 3,500 sq ft, new construction — without any prompting. This is the highest-value signal in the call. Holland responds with 'Uh-huh' and lets it pass. The immediate battery need is the entry point; the new-home solar project is the real prize. A rep who catches this anchors it explicitly: 'So we're solving the immediate problem today AND getting you set up for the big system when the house is ready — let me make sure the specialist knows both.' Holland never registers it.

key_moments.quote: I'm just tired of the EcoFlow. The problem with EcoFlow is they can't be charged by a generator. And so I want and you can't charge it with a generator.
key_moments.stewart_read: This is the functional equivalent of bill pain for an off-grid customer. Kelly is not complaining about a utility bill — she is describing a system failure that leaves her without power for three to four days during cloud cover. The pain is concrete, urgent, and emotionally loaded. Holland responds with 'Okay. Perfect.' and moves to a supervisor hold. The flip was available: 'So right now your backup plan is a generator that your system won't even accept — that's the problem we're solving.' Holland drops it entirely.

key_moments.quote: Accel wants me to pay $90,000 to bring current to my house.
key_moments.stewart_read: Kelly hands Holland the clearest pain anchor in the call — a $90,000 grid-connection quote that is the entire reason she is off-grid and battery-dependent. Holland responds: 'Yeah. That's crazy. Yeah. Wow. Yeah. I forget. You said you're all the way off the grid.' Acknowledged, then dropped. The flip was available and would have been effortless: 'Ninety thousand dollars just to get connected — that's exactly why what we're building for you matters.' Instead Holland moves immediately to the bill-collection requirement, which lands as absurd given what Kelly just said.

key_moments.quote: to get, like, any credit or anything to go along with the battery, you'll just need to have a credit score above 670. So do you know if that's the case for you?
key_moments.stewart_read: Holland applies the standard credit qualifier to a customer who has already said she is off-grid and will be paying cash. The qualifier is designed for financed solar installs. Kelly's pushback — 'Why do I need a credit score? I'm not financing' — is entirely reasonable. Holland recovers correctly ('You're totally fine. You don't have to.') but the damage to credibility is already done. This is not a protocol violation in the codex sense; it is a misapplication of the qualifier to a customer profile the standard script does not anticipate.
